data.title: Prenetics Global: On Course To Hit Profitability Amid Globalization Drive
data.description:
Shares of consumer health brand, Prenetics Global Limited (PRE), have surged 12.35% since my last article on the company and are up 344.64% (YoY). Portfolio reorganization and capital reallocation contributed to the quarterly revenue upside of 248.6% (YoY) in Q4 2025 and +202% (YoY) in the FY 2025. I believe this stock is still a buy since its breakout and growth story is bound to continue into 2026 and beyond, especially after divesting all its non-core assets to focus on the global scaling of IM8. The company is also heading towards profitability with a robust balance sheet.

After attaining an annual revenue milestone of $92.4 million in the FY 2025, PRE is projecting to generate about $180 million to $200 million (a midpoint of $190 million) for the FY 2026. I believe this is achievable since the primary IM8 business hit an annual revenue run rate of $120 million in FY 2025 after its launch in December 2024. Further, this revenue guidance of 105.63% (YoY) coincides with the analyst revenue forward growth rate of 114.74% (YoY).

Another aspect to consider is the profitability concerns since PRE is yet to break even.

Sec filings also show the company’s liquidity is at $171 million, and a total cash position of almost $100 million (with no debt) after selling its 35% stake at Insighta for $70 million (which I predicted in my previous article). This liquidity reduces PRE’s chances of diluting its stock to seek working capital. In its Q4 2025 earnings call, the management estimated that it hopes to raise its gross margins from 60% to 63% over the next 12 to 18 months (by Q3 2027). Since the revenue guidance over the next 12 months is set at $190 million, I will multiply it by 0.37, i.e., (1-0.63), to get the cost of goods, which equals to $70.3 million. It represents an increase of 43.76% (YoY) from $48.9 million posted by PRE as its COGS in the FY 2025. While raising the revenue guidance to $300 million by 2027 with the gross margin of 63% will lift the COGS to $111 million, it will mean sales will have surpassed COGS by over 170%. This informs part of the management’s assertion that it will be profitable by Q4 2027.

Further, the company is considering deploying novel AI tools that will also help drive down costs. Administrative expenses (SG&A) grew 46.57% (YoY) in FY 2025 and have been the largest cost component for PRE. Over time, these expenses have been attributed to high marketing and customer acquisition activities as well as inventory growth. The company stated in its Q4 2025 report that it was looking to use the “latest AI tools and systems” in every function of the organization, including digital marketing. The addition of key celebrities to its IM8 brand as both ambassadors and shareholders means the company has access to over 200 million followers worldwide. It’s also interesting to note how these celebrity athletes (IM8 ambassadors) come from different sports, giving Prenetics a wide market reach. The inclusion of AI in digital marketing will be an integral part in reaching this likely customer base in 2026 and beyond.

Then again, IM8’s partnership with Vitamin Angels is a key step towards extending the scale of Prenetics’ global agenda. We are witnessing the evolution of IM8 from serving as a health supplement to seekers of performance and longevity, to a supplement that can help fight malnutrition, among “pregnant women, infants, and children below the age of 5 years.” It will now not be just about building strength and agility (while using this supplement) but also about saving lives. I believe the company’s mission has not changed; it just got higher through this CSR project. By 2027, Prenetics hopes to have reached at least 400,000 of this vulnerable population. We are looking at a stronger IM8 brand and a greater penetration into over 65 countries and more than 74 million underserved women and children.”

Valuation

I will use the EV/Sales to evaluate PRE due to its high top-line projection despite the company not being profitable. I am factoring in its high growth prospects that I believe will define its future as far as this analysis is concerned.

PRE’s forward EV/Sales ratio stands at 1.20X. To calculate the enterprise value, I will multiply the forward sales value of $190 million by the forward EV/sales multiple (1.20X) to get $228 million. The forward market capitalization will be obtained by adding the cash position that sits at $99.3 million, which equals to $327.3 million. We have to keep in mind that PRE has 0 debt, which will be excluded from this calculation. Total shares outstanding for PRE as of Q1 2026 was 16 million shares, meaning that the forward price for the company is $20.46 per share. As of this writing, PRE’s share price stood at $17.83, meaning that it is undervalued by about 15%.

The sales growth projection by Prenetics to approximately $300 million in the FY 2027 represents an increase of 57.89% (YoY) from the forward sales guidance of $190 million for the FY 2026. It means the EV is likely to hit $360 million in 2027. With a cash position of at least $100 million, PRE’s market cap may sit at $460 million in 2027 (without debt) and a future share price of $28.75. It means that PRE has a possible upside above 60% (over the next 24 months).

I will compare the performance of PRE with some of its peers using the EV/Sales multiple.

EV/Sales multiple Fwd. Sales Fwd. EV Future Market Cap Future Share price Current share price Valuation difference
PRE 1.20X $190 million $228 million $327.3 million $20.46 $17.83 15% (YoY) undervalued
Herbalife Ltd. (HLF) 0.68X $5.34 billion $3.63 billion $1.8 billion $17.41 $17.02 +2.3% (YoY) trading at fair valuation

Nu Skin Enterprises, Inc. (NUS)

 0.32X $1.43 billion $457.6 million $696.23 million $13.95 $7.90 +76.58% (YoY)

While PRE has been undervalued by about 15% (YoY), the tabulated analysis shows that Nu Skin has a possible upside of approximately 77% (YoY). Herbalife is trading at a fair valuation ahead of its Q1 2026 financial earnings report on May 6, 2026. In my previous analysis, PRE’s forward sales for the FY 2026 were estimated at $195 million, indicating that the current guidance shows a drop of 2.63% (relative to the midpoint of $190 million given recently by the company).

Risk

This valuation takes into consideration Prenetics’ sales guidance ranging from $180 million to $200 million. Failure to attain this projected sales growth will harm this valuation.

I have also noted that Prenetics is yet to break even in terms of its bottom line, but has targeted Q4 2027 when it will hit profitability. I will await its next earnings release, possibly by June 2026, to update my analysis.

Bottom Line

I have maintained my buy rating for PRE with a possible upside of 15% (YoY) and +60% (over the next 24 months) contingent on the attainment of their revenue guidance of $300 million in FY 2027. The company's globalization agenda is also taking shape through its partnership with renowned sportsmen and, recently, with Vitamin Angels. It also hopes to leverage AI to help absorb part of the costs while seeking to hit profitability by Q4 2027.

data.title: Tesla, Intel, IBM To Report Earnings Next Week
data.description:
Get ahead of the market by subscribing to Seeking Alpha's Wall Street Week Ahead, a preview of key events scheduled for the coming week. The newsletter keeps you informed of the biggest stories set to make headlines, including upcoming IPOs, investor days, earnings reports, and conference presentations.

Wall Street's major averages jumped on Friday to record highs after Iran declared the Strait of Hormuz open for commercial vessels. President Donald Trump, however, said the naval blockade will remain in effect. The Ports and Maritime Organization of the Islamic Republic of Iran said the Strait of Hormuz is now “completely open” for all commercial vessels for the rest of the Lebanon ceasefire timeline. This comes after Israel and Lebanon announced a 10-day ceasefire on Thursday.

The economic calendar seems muted with only a few important data releases in the coming week. Retail sales data will be out on Tuesday. S&P Global’s PMI data for April is scheduled for Thursday alongside the jobs data.

Earnings spotlight: Monday: Steel Dynamics (STLD), Alaska Air (ALK). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Tuesday: GE Aerospace (GE), 3M (MMM), Haliburton (HAL), United Airlines (UAL). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Wednesday: Tesla (TSLA), Philip Morris (PM), IBM (IBM), Boeing (BA). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Thursday: Intel (INTC), American Express (AXP), Gilead (GLD), Blackstone (BX). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Friday: P&G (PG). See the full earnings calendar.

Volatility watch: Car rental stocks Avis Budget (CAR) and Hertz Global (HTZ) are set up for another volatile week of trading based on options volume. The most overbought stocks per their 14-day relative strength index include Mega Fortune (MGRT), Avanos Medical (AVNS), and Willow Lane Acquisition (WLAC). The most oversold stocks per their 14-day Relative Strength Index include PepGen (PEPG), MiMedx (MDXG), and WW International (WW). Short interest is elevated on Lucid Group (LCID) and C3.ai (AI) again.

IPO watch: Convenience store chain Yesway (YSWY), healthcare REIT National Healthcare Properties (HLTC), and Elmet Group (ELMT) are expected to price their IPOs and begin trading. The IPO lockup periods expire on blocks of shares of Charming Medical (MCTA) and Bgin Blockchain (BGIN). The quiet period ends on Ursb (URSB) to free up analysts to post ratings.

Dividend watch: Companies that have an ex-dividend date coming next week include Caterpillar (CAT), Dell Technologies (DELL), Lowe's Companies (LOW), and CVS Health (CVS).

Tesla Earnings Preview: Tesla is expected to report first-quarter earnings of $0.36 per share on revenue of $22.34B. Analysts are bearish on the company ahead of its results, predominantly due to its Q1 deliveries. The EV major produced 408,386 vehicles and delivered 358,023, representing a gap of over 50,000 cars. The deliveries still were below Bloomberg’s estimate of 365,645. According to UBS, the days of inventory grew to 35 from 21 in the prior quarter, the highest days of inventory level recorded since UBS began tracking this statistic. Analysts view this as a clear sign of a demand bottleneck that Tesla is facing. Meanwhile, they also believe that the firm’s financials remain unimpressive, with negligible free cash flow growth and no return to robust growth in sight.

Intel Earnings Preview: Intel is seen reporting Q1 EPS of $0.01 and revenue of $12.39B. Analysts are cautious ahead of the earnings, with the majority of their pessimism focused on the firm’s Foundry business. In 2025, Intel Foundry posted $17.8B of segment revenue but a $10.3B operating loss, and external Foundry and assembly/test revenue was just $307M. It has yet to secure meaningful external customer wins, while continued negative free cash flow is fueling concerns around capital allocation and execution, an analyst said. However, the management expects Foundry revenue to rise in double digits quarter-over-quarter, “helped by continued mix shift to EUV wafers and Intel 18A pricing." Within the Intel Products, the company had forecast a more pronounced revenue decline in CCG than in DCAI.

data.title: Nasdaq Up By 60% Since Liberation Day Selloff And By 100% In 3 Years - People Are Just Having Fun
data.description:
While the Nasdaq gained 100%, the economy grew by 15%, not adjusted for inflation. Who needs cryptos when stocks are so much fun.

The Nasdaq Composite Index jumped 1.5% on Friday, wrapping up its 13th consecutive day of gains, the longest such streak since 1992, according to Dow Jones Market Data. It jumped by 17.7% in those 13 trading days and set a new high earlier this week.

But the bigger thing: since the bottom of the Liberation Day selloff on April 8, 2025, so in a little over a year, the Nasdaq has gained 60%. This may also have been a record going back to the neolithic period. Or maybe it was just kind of normal or whatever. At any rate, a lot of fun was had by all. To the moon!

Since May 1, 2023, in less than three years, the Nasdaq Composite has doubled; it gained 100%, despite the Liberation Day trough in between. Probably just normal or whatever. The S&P 500 gained 71% during that period.

Over the same period of less than three years, when the Nasdaq soared by 100% and the S&P 500 by 71%, the US economy grew by 15%, not adjusted for inflation (nominal GDP growth). Not any kind of disconnect whatsoever. Nothing to see here, folks.

The S&P 500 Index jumped 1.2% on Friday and by 13% in those 13 trading days. Since the bottom of the Liberation Day selloff on April 8, 2025, in a little over a year, it gained 43%, possibly also a record for the S&P 500 since the neolithic period, or maybe just normal or whatever.

The WSJ ran an article this morning that was years too late, but it did nail it: “It’s Getting Harder to Tell Investing from Gambling, and it’s Not Your Fault.”

No one cares anymore about anything, as long as this stuff just keeps going up, driven by others that are buying it with the same attitude.

There have always been overlaps between gambling and investing, in some periods more than in other periods. It comes and goes in waves. And the distinction has never been clear. It doesn’t have to do with risk-taking but with the reasons behind that risk-taking, maybe.

During the dot-com bubble, the distinction for those stocks involved faded entirely. Then the Nasdaq Composite collapsed by 78% from March 2000 to October 2002, and thousands of companies vanished.

But that was then, and this is now. Crypto trading is gambling by definition. There is nothing else there. And now people equate cryptos and stocks, and Wall Street wants to put cryptos into retirement accounts. For people who bought into crypto, gambling and investing have fused completely. And big brokerage houses piled into it to make money off that gambling.

But who needs cryptos when stocks are even more fun? Bitcoin has plunged by 38% since its high in October last year. That’s not fun. Where’s the moon?

Much more fun to pile into stocks, such as the stock of former sneaker retailer Allbirds (BIRD), that had gone public in November 2021 and the shares exploded and gave the company a value of $4 billion and then collapsed by 99.5%. In March, the company sold its intellectual property, including the brand, for $39 million, and by April 15, the shares were down to about $2.50 and the market cap to about $21 million, when the company announced that it would pivot to AI infrastructure. And it said it lined up $50 million of potential investment that is actually a death-spiral convertible stock offering, upon which the stock spiked by 890% to $24.31. To the moon. It has since then given up over half of that. Not even cryptos can do that routinely. And the stock market is full of this stuff. People are just having fun.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Roblox's Set-Up For Long Term Success
data.description:
Investment Summary

Roblox Corporation (NYSE: RBLX) is one of the up-and-coming gaming platforms for users of all ages. While still in its early developmental phase in exploring different gaming categories for expansion, RBLX is positioned to capture a significant portion of the market share based on its unique platform moat.

I believe that while RBLX's share price has fallen over the past few months as investor sentiments weaken on the safety of their platform, this is more of a temporary fall and RBLX represents a long-term investment opportunity driven by their market penetration plans and growing user base.

Business Overview

Roblox was founded in 2004 and headquartered in California, United States.

Their core operations focus on providing users with access to a vast library of creator-generated games and digital experiences. Anyone under the age of 23 would have definitely heard of RBLX as it is one of the most popular games of its time.

Business Model

RBLX operates on a 3-sided flywheel. Users spend real-life money in exchange for Robux (in-game currency), Robux is then used to purchase in-game items. RBLX will capture a portion of each transaction of Robux before passing down the earnings back to game creators.

RBLX does not pay creators for making games. Instead, RBLX incentivizes creators to do so through the creator rewards program and the developer exchange program, where creators are rewarded for creating games that capture users' retention, engagement, and purchases. This has been successful for RBLX, as is evident from their library of over 14 million game experiences.

Competitive moat

What differentiates RBLX from other traditional gaming companies also lies in the scale of its content ecosystem. To put into perspective as to how big their game portfolio is: Steam, one of the largest game distribution platforms, has only around 130,000 games. Fortnite, a game similar to Roblox with its creator driven content model, has only 487,000 game experiences. This means that RBLX's content library is almost collectively exhaustive in the range of experiences available (14 million), catering to a highly diverse global user base.

In addition to this, what makes RBLX even more distinct, is its accessibility. The platform operates seamlessly on handheld devices without requiring high-end hardware or gaming consoles as compared to Fortnite (RBLX's main competitors).

The minimum supported devices that RBLX has listed are relatively low-spec, including entry level smartphones like the iPhone 6s, and older PCs with low processing power and memory requirements.

Mobile penetration rate globally is 97.8%, compared to gaming consoles at 18.9% and computers at around 58.3%. As a result, RBLX is able to tap into a much broader global user base, especially among younger demographics who primarily access games via mobile devices.

In contrast, competitors such as Fortnite and other high-fidelity games are constrained by hardware requirements. This creates a structural advantage for RBLX in scaling its platform globally.

Of course this brings the question: If RBLX is capable of running on such low specs, would their graphics, design, and overall gameplay be subpar compared to high-fidelity games?

The answer lies in the Scalable Lightweight Interactive Model (SLIM) that RBLX runs on.

SLIM basically creates multiple simplified, optimized representations of any object or model in a developer's world and stores them on the server to be retrieved at runtime. This allows for users with lower-spec devices to render a lightweight SLIM representation of the object tailored to their hardware capabilities, ensuring smoother performance. Meanwhile, users with higher-spec devices will render a higher quality model/object.

RBLX's SLIM technology allows the platform to serve both low- and high-spec devices effectively. While it enables smooth performance on lower-end hardware, it also preserves visual quality for users with more powerful systems. Thus, Roblox remains accessible without compromising the experience to users on both end of the spectrum in terms of hardware.

RBLX's falling share price

RBLX's share price has declined significantly since its 52 week high of $150.59 to $57.35 as of 19-March 2026, representing a decrease of 62% .

This stems from 2 main issues that they currently face: 1. Child safety Lawsuits and Investigations into RBLX as there were previous cases of child exploitation on the platform, and 2. Decreasing Users regarding their new implementation of safety features.

Over-exaggeration of Lawsuits and Perceived downside of safety features

Addressing the first concern, RBLX faces lawsuits filed by Louisiana and other state authorities alleging the platform of exposing children to sexual content, exploitation, and online predators. The Australian government also has sought an urgent meeting with the company surrounding child safety on the platform.

In recognition of these potential implications, RBLX was quick to act, rolling out a three-layered safety framework. From late Q4 2025 to 31 Jan 2026, almost 45% of global Daily Average Users (DAU) has undergone a mandatory age check using Persona's age estimation model. This model achieves an absolute mean error of 1.4 years for minors under 18, meaning the age prediction model is, on average, 1.4 years off a child's real age.

Beyond age verification, RBLX also implemented advanced text filters, behavioral analysis, leveraging play patterns, social graphs, and economic activities to further refine their age estimation.

The final layer within RBLX's framework involves segregating users by age groups. This ensures that users of a certain age are only able to interact with people in similar age groups, reducing the risks of predatory behaviors.

Collectively, these measures improve RBLX's ability to prevent age misrepresentation and protect younger users.

While there are certainly loopholes within RBLX's newly implemented systems, with such fast-acting and deep integrations in place, it makes the lawsuit of negligence harder to substantiate. Moreover, RBLX stated that they intend to further invest in safety infrastructures in order to address regulatory concerns, strengthen user trust, and support sustainable growth as the platform scales.

Despite these improvements, the market's sentiment towards RBLX's newly implemented safety features has been largely negative. Investors are worried that by limiting users within their own age groups, it reduces the platform's core appeal. After all, RBLX is a platform where many experiences are inherently social and designed to be played together, so given such restrictions, it decreases engagement among users, potentially leading to lower play time and reduced spending on RBLX.

However, a key feature I believe that they have potentially missed out on would be Trusted Connections. Trusted connections allow age-checked users of 13 and older to interact with ID verified trusted individuals after adding each other. This feature aims to remove a layer of restriction in their safety implementations, so as to not completely confine users to their age groups only but also allow for meaningful interactions with others that they trust. By introducing trusted connections, RBLX aims to strike a balance between safety and social freedom, loosening age segmentation while maintaining safeguards.

Furthermore, as RBLX continues to expand on the existing safety infrastructure, the censorship of RBLX is expected to ease over time. Certain markets, such as Algeria, Egypt and Iraq, would gradually lift the restriction on RBLX, giving them a larger TAM, supporting renewed user growth and monetization, contrary to the market's sentiment.

While safety features introduce friction into RBLX's player base, RBLX's continued investment in safety infrastructure is not only about restrictions but also optimization. Features such as trusted connections are in place to ensure that core social interactions are still in place. As RBLX continues to explore methods of verification and safeguards, we can expect safety features to improve over time and become less intrusive, reducing negative impact on users' trust.

RBLX's expansion into Novel Games and High-Fidelity games

Amidst all the controversy and skepticism, an important expansion plan that RBLX has in mind lacked attention.

RBLX's expansion into novel and high-fidelity game is interesting because RBLX is built on providing users experiences without having the need to purchase them. This itself is fundamentally different from other games as their main source of revenue is from users purchasing the game itself, whereas RBLX actively discourages creators from putting their games behind a paywall but instead uses more creative methods for revenue generation, such as passes, developer products, and special paid access in experiences.

RBLX's platform addresses a core pain point within the gaming industry: Game discovery. According to Bain and Company, the top 10 game titles on gaming platforms captures 50-60% of total revenue for active games. This level of concentration means that player attention is heavily skewed towards a small number of titles, making it harder for newer games to gain visibility and be discovered especially when they are behind paywalls. By removing paywalls, RBLX makes game discovery much easier, as this lowers the barriers to entry for players and developers, allowing users to sample a wider variety of games without restriction.

The market also is concerned that RBLX does not have the graphics nor technical capability to compete with high-fidelity games since RBLX's original design prioritized accessibility. However, this concern may be overstated. Survey data indicated that only 7% of gamers perceive high-quality graphics as the most important factor, while the number 1 reason (at 22%) cited was gameplay.

This suggests that RBLX actually does not have to compete directly on visual fidelity to win user engagement but rather adopt a more novel gameplay experience, creativity and social experience where graphics are less critical.

Furthermore, RBLX has already begun collaborating with giant game corporations such as SEGA corporation.

RBLX plans to develop a themed experience with SEGA, bringing the world of Like a Dragon into RBLX as a licensed experience. Like a Dragon is known for its mature storytelling and exploration of complex themes such as love, humanity, and betrayal, catering to their expansion plans of reaching a broader and more mature demographic. RBLX plans to continue developing more of such experiences through their licensing manager, acting as a central gateway which IP holders can grant creators and developers the rights to build within their established universes and create even more experiences similar to Like a Dragon. This established model also has partnerships with Lionsgate, the world's largest independent film and TV studio, Netflix, and Kodansha with plenty of opportunities to add more in the future.

RBLX's user base has also displayed a structural shift in demographics, with users aged 13+ increasing significantly year over year, accounting for 61% of its total user base. The 17-24 age group is now the single largest segment on the platform at 26%, while adults over 25 account for 19% of the cohort.

Critically, this is not just a headcount story, verified adults (18+) monetize 40% higher than younger users while having penetration rates of less than 10%. A large runway for growth paired with RBLX's License manager's ability to bring mature, narrative-rich IP onto the platform, RBLX is well positioned to deepen their engagement and wallet share among this cohort. Thus, RBLX is able to convert a demographic tailwind into durable revenue growth, which the market underappreciates.

Valuations

When forecasting RBLX's revenue, it is slightly tricky because when a user purchases their in-game currency, it is realized over 2.33 years rather than being immediately realized. Hence, a separate "Adjusted Earnings per year" had to be created to find the real value generated by in game purchases in that specific year.

Furthermore, I broke down the daily average users into their respective age categories and forecasted them forward, with users aged 17+ to have a more significant growth compared to users below 17, as RBLX aims to expand into more novel and high fidelity games that cater to that age range.

Line items such as infrastructure and safety are forecasted to match the cost per DAU. With RBLX needing to address the safety issue on their platform, I forecasted the cost to increase by 10% and plateau to 2% as safety infrastructure matures.

Furthermore, developer exchange fees are forecasted to increase into the future as more developers try to break into the high fidelity and novel game category, so it is highly likely that overall developer headcount would increase into the future alongside the player base that RBLX is actively trying to cater to.

I arrived at an implied share price of $101.08, a 67.24% upside from RBLX's current share price on its US ADR, proving that RBLX is undervalued.

Risks and Concerns

The main risk regarding RBLX would of course be the safety on its platform, after all, a large portion of their user base is underage. Hence, platform safety is a real risk to the company, since if not handled properly would result in a declining DAU leading to lower revenue generated.

That said, RBLX has demonstrated a proactive stance in addressing these concerns. The company has meaningfully ramped up investment in its safety infrastructure, rolling out features such as parental controls, age verification, and AI-driven content moderation.

These efforts signal that management treats platform safety not merely as a compliance checkbox, but as a core pillar of sustainable growth. As long as Roblox continues to iterate on these systems and stays ahead of the evolving regulatory landscape, I believe the safety risk — while real — is manageable and does not fundamentally impair the long-term investment thesis.

Conclusion

Overall, I believe that RBLX is an excellent company with a well-defined and unique moat among its gaming peers. Combined with a growing user base and a significant tailwind in expansion into novel and high-fidelity games, I believe RBLX presents itself as an attractive company for investors.

data.title: PayPal: A Contrarian Buy At 9x Earnings
data.description:
What is interesting about Paypal?

PayPal Holdings, Inc. (NASDAQ: PYPL) closed on April 11 at $46, roughly 20% above its February lows but still 42% below last summer's $79.50 high. You know what happened: a Q4 miss, a confusing CEO exit, and guidance that scared everyone off. The stock cratered 19% on February 3 and has bounced around the mid-$40s ever since.

What you get at today's price is a company trading at roughly 9x forward earnings and 5.4x trailing EV/EBITDA. That's the cheapest PayPal has ever been. And actually, the business underneath all the noise actually had a pretty solid 2025.

I'm going to walk through the numbers in detail below, but here is the short version: PayPal's FY2025 10-K shows $33.2 billion in revenue, $5.31 in non-GAAP EPS (up 14% year-on-year), $6.4 billion in adjusted free cash flow, and Venmo growing 20% to $1.7 billion. Management plowed $6 billion into buybacks and started the company's first dividend.

My 18-24 month target is $76 (65% upside). I also have a 12-month target of $55 (20%) that does not assume anything gets better, just that the narrative stabilizes. I'll show the math for both throughout the piece.

The Business in 2026

Quick reminder of what PayPal actually does today: 439 million active accounts in roughly 200 countries. Four revenue engines: the branded checkout button (highest margin), Braintree unbranded processing (enterprise merchants, thinner margin), Venmo (P2P turned commerce platform, growing fast), and buy-now-pay-later ($40+ billion in TPV last year, still scaling).

And then there's the stablecoin business, which barely gets mentioned in sell-side coverage but is moving quickly. PYUSD is backed 1:1 by Paxos-held dollar deposits and Treasuries. In March, PayPal pushed it into 70 countries and separately connected Venmo to cross-border transfers in 90 markets. Simply Wall St called these moves potentially transformative for engagement.

What Happened in Q4 (and All Year)

The quarter and the full year deserve to be discussed separately, because they tell opposite stories.

Q4 was bad. $8.68 billion in revenue, which missed consensus by $120 million. EPS of $1.23 (non-GAAP) missed by about 5%. The real damage was in branded checkout, where currency-neutral growth dropped to 1% from 5% the quarter before. US consumer softness, international weakness, and some competitive share loss all contributed.

Full year was something different. Revenue $33.2 billion, +4%. Non-GAAP operating income $6.38 billion, +9%. Operating margin 19.2%, up 87 bps. EPS $5.31, +14%. Transaction margin dollars (ex-interest on customer balances) grew 6% to $14.2 billion. $6 billion in buybacks took the share count down roughly 7%. First-ever quarterly dividend of $0.14. If you compare this to FY2024 (18.4% margins, $4.65 EPS), the improvement is obvious.

There is something that needs to be highlighted: GAAP free cash flow fell 18% to $5.6 billion. That's because of how PayPal accounts for BNPL receivables, originating them and then selling them off at different times during the quarter. The adjusted figure ($6.4 billion) removes that timing mismatch. I use the adjusted number in my model but I watch the GAAP number closely because if that gap widens as BNPL grows, it becomes a real concern.

The CEO Situation

Alex Chriss had only been CEO for about two and a half years when the board replaced him with Enrique Lores on February 3, effective March 1. Chriss did some good things: he brought in new people, pushed the "price-to-value" strategy in Braintree, stabilized margins. But American Banker reported that the board lost patience over branded checkout, and looking at Q4's numbers I can probably see why!

Lores is an interesting choice. He ran HP Inc. (NYSE: HPQ) for over six years and managed a real transformation there, taking a PC company into services and subscriptions. What I find encouraging is that he's not parachuting in cold. He's been on PayPal's board since 2021 and chaired it from July 2024. He saw the problems up close. David Dorman took the independent chair role.

At this valuation, I think the CEO transition is an asymmetric bet. The stock already prices in management uncertainty. If Lores shows progress on branded checkout in the May or August earnings reports, the shares rerate. If he doesn't, I'm not sure the stock has much further to fall from 9x earnings.

Three Main Catalysts

Venmo

Venmo went from about $900 million in revenue in 2021 to $1.7 billion in FY2025. Monthly active accounts hit 67 million (up 7%). Debit card TPV grew over 50% in Q4 alone. And in late March, Venmo went international for the first time, connecting to PayPal accounts in 90 countries.

There's survey data in the press release showing 59% of payment app users would consolidate to one app if it worked for international transfers. That's a large addressable pool. The World Bank puts remittance flows to developing countries at $685+ billion annually, and Western Union still charges 5-7% on many corridors. Venmo at zero or near-zero fees (PayPal is waiving international fees through August) is a legitimate competitive entry.

Management has publicly targeted $2 billion in Venmo revenue by 2027. Debit card TPV compounds at 20%+ through that horizon, Pay with Venmo merchant volume at roughly double that pace, and the number of merchants accepting it grew 50% last year. I believe the $2 billion target is realistic, possibly conservative.

What does that mean for PYPL stock? At $2 billion, Venmo would be about 6% of total revenue but growing 4-5x faster than the parent. That kind of mix shift matters for rerating. And a crude sum-of-the-parts: $2 billion in revenue at 3x (a discount to every peer) implies $6 billion of standalone value, or about $6-7 per PYPL share that I don't think the market gives credit for today.

PYUSD Stablecoin Infrastructure

Circulating supply blew through $4.3 billion (roughly 4x from eight months ago). But the bigger deal is PYUSDx, a framework PayPal built with MoonPay and M0 that lets developers create their own stablecoins collateralized by PYUSD. This is PayPal trying to become plumbing for stablecoin commerce. Not a revenue driver yet, but strategically it is a space where none of the legacy processors are present.

Fastlane, BNPL, and Offline Commerce

Fastlane is the guest checkout product, and the results are strong: merchants report about 50% conversion improvement. BNPL did over $40 billion in TPV growing 20%+. Tap to Pay plus debit card grew 50%+ in Q4. Still small in the mix, but these are the segments that will matter in two or three years when analysts ask where growth came from.

Connecting the dots: if Venmo adds $300M+ in incremental revenue by FY2027, BNPL volume reaches $48-50 billion in FY2026 and approaches $60 billion in FY2027, and the offline channel keeps compounding at 50%+, then 5-6% consolidated revenue growth for FY2027-28 is entirely achievable without branded checkout needing to do anything heroic. Low single-digit branded growth plus these faster segments gets you there.

2026: An investment year, but I am okay with it!

The 2026 guidance was not great. Non-GAAP EPS: low-single-digit decline to roughly flat versus $5.31. TM$ flat to slightly down. OpEx +3%. FCF at least $6 billion. Buybacks $6 billion. CapEx $1 billion.

Consensus had modeled +8% EPS growth. Instead: possible EPS decline plus departing CEO plus withdrawn 2027 targets. Stock dropped 19%. Makes sense.

What I'd argue is that the guidance reflects a specific choice, not a deterioration. PayPal disclosed that roughly 3 points of TM$ growth are being eaten by investment spending, split between branded checkout improvements (better button placement, upstream BNPL, co-marketing) and Venmo loyalty and agentic commerce. Call it $400-450 million of incremental spend that depresses near-term margins. That is deliberate. One quarter before the Q4 report, in Q3, PayPal had raised guidance and posted 7% revenue growth. The deceleration was sharp and the timing was awful, but the spending decision itself wasn't irrational.

So the question is: can branded checkout actually stop bleeding? I think yes, and here's why I'm not just guessing. Fastlane already delivers 50% better conversion where deployed, but penetration across the merchant base is still early. PayPal has said biometric authentication in branded checkout is targeting about 50% adoption (up from much less). Every point of biometric adoption reduces cart abandonment at essentially zero marginal cost. Management is also paying for better placement of the PayPal button on the payment sheet and moving the BNPL option earlier in the shopping funnel so consumers see it before they check out. This is not speculative technology investment. It's tactical execution with measurable results.

I expect branded checkout to return to low-to-mid single-digit currency-neutral growth by late 2026 or early 2027, mostly from Fastlane and presentment changes. That's what my DCF assumes. Nothing more.

And I'll say this: the decision to invest through a bad quarter was the right call strategically even though it was terrible timing optically. The alternative was to protect the earnings print while branded checkout slowly eroded. That would have been the safe, short-term-oriented move, and the stock probably would have ended up in the same place or lower once the erosion showed up in future quarters anyway. The spending headwind is finite, and if even half of it sticks, TM$ growth reaccelerates to the 7-9% range PayPal was targeting at its investor day. If it doesn't stick, you still have $6 billion in free cash flow and a company buying back 14% of its market cap annually.

Valuation

Comparable Analysis

Here's how PayPal stacks up against its peer group as of this week:

I'll skip the Visa Inc. (NYSE: V) / Mastercard Incorporated (NYSE: MA) comparison because they're card networks, not processors, and deserve a structural premium. The better peer group is Block, Inc. (NYSE: XYZ), Fiserv, Inc. (NASDAQ: FISV), and Adyen N.V. (OTC: ADYEY). Median forward P/E in that group: about 13x. Median EV/EBITDA: about 10x. PayPal trades at a 30-50% discount to both despite superior free cash flow generation.

I use 13x forward P/E and 10x EV/EBITDA as my target multiples. At 13x FY2025 non-GAAP EPS of $5.31 you get $69. At 10x $7.35 billion in trailing EBITDA, adding $3.2 billion net cash, dividing by 936 million shares, you get $82. Average: $76.

DCF Walkthrough

I want to be specific about the assumptions because I think transparent models are more useful than DCFs that just spit out a number.

Revenue: FY2026 at $34.5 billion (4% growth, in line with guidance). FY2027 at 5%, FY2028 at 6% (as investments start paying off), FY2029 back to 5%. PayPal did 4% in FY2025 with a terrible Q4, so these aren't stretch targets.

Margins: I cut non-GAAP operating margin to 18.0% in FY2026, which is 120 bps below the 19.2% they just reported. That's the investment year drag. Recovery to 18.5% in FY2027, 19.0% in FY2028-29. No margin expansion beyond what they already achieved.

Tax/D&A/capex: 20% tax rate (matches FY2025 non-GAAP actuals). D&A from $1,050M to $1,150M over the projection period. CapEx starts at $1,000M (guided) and climbs to $1,100M. $200M annual working capital charge for BNPL growth.

WACC: 10.0%. The bottom-up math gives about 9.6% (4.3% risk-free (10-year Treasury as of last week), 5.0-5.5% equity risk premium, 1.25 beta, after-tax debt cost 3.6% per the 10-K, 21% debt-to-capital using $11.6 billion in debt against ~$43 billion market cap). I round up to 10% to give myself a cushion for the CEO transition.

Terminal growth: 2.0%. Below nominal GDP. Payments is a competitive market, and I do not think PayPal grows at GDP forever.

Discounted FCFs sum to $17.0 billion. Terminal value: $6.1 billion terminal-year FCF divided by 8% (WACC minus terminal growth) gives $76.2 billion, or $52.1 billion present value. That's 75% of total enterprise value, which is normal for a mature cash flow generator.

EV: $69.1 billion. Net cash: $3.2 billion. Shares: 936 million. Implied price: $77.

Price Targets

Blending comps ($76) and DCF ($77): $76, about 65% upside over 18-24 months.

12-month target: $55. 10x forward on roughly $5.20 in FY2026 EPS (low-single-digit decline from $5.31). Still cheaper than every processor peer except Fiserv. $55 is approximately where PYPL traded before the Q4 print, so getting there just requires the bleeding to stop. About 20% upside.

The Buyback Math

PayPal retired $6 billion in stock last year. 86 million shares, roughly 7% of the float. At $43 billion market cap, that's a 14% buyback yield. They've guided the same for FY2026 and target 70-80% of FCF toward capital returns.

What gets me is the price they're buying at. 9x earnings. If the stock does nothing for two years but the company keeps buying at this multiple, the per-share economics improve substantially just from the shrinking denominator. Add the $0.14/quarter dividend ($0.56 annualized, about 1.2% yield) and total shareholder return from capital allocation alone could run 15-16% per year.

Risks

Branded checkout keeps losing ground. This is the risk that matters most. If Q4's 1% FX-neutral growth was not a trough but the beginning of something worse, then the company's best margin product is in secular decline. Apple Inc.'s (NASDAQ: AAPL) Apple Pay and merchant-native checkouts are real threats.

The new CEO doesn't deliver. New leadership during an investment year with no 2027 guidance creates genuine uncertainty. Two weak quarters under Lores, and sentiment could deteriorate further.

Competition on every front. Stripe and Adyen on enterprise processing. Cash App on the younger user base. Apple Pay in physical stores. Low switching costs for merchants and consumers.

Lawsuits. Multiple securities fraud class actions pending (April 2026 deadlines). Typical post-decline legal noise, but it eats management time.

Consumer spending risk. Branded checkout tracks US discretionary spending. A recession stretches out the recovery timeline.

GAAP vs. adjusted FCF. $5.6B vs. $6.4B. The gap is BNPL timing. Legit adjustment, but worth monitoring as the book grows.

Conclusion

PayPal is at the cheapest multiple it has ever traded at. A business producing $6 billion in annual free cash flow, buying back 14% of its equity base every year, processing $1.8 trillion in payments, growing Venmo 20%, and expanding its stablecoin network globally. All available at 9x earnings.

I don’t want to understate the issues here:

  • Branded checkout at 1% growth is a legitimate concern;
  • A brand-new CEO in an investment year creates uncertainty;
  • The relentless competition.

But I keep doing the math, and the math keeps saying the same thing. At $46, the downside is protected by cash flow and buybacks even if nothing improves. And if Lores manages to steady branded checkout and Venmo keeps on its trajectory, the stock has 65-90% upside to a range of reasonable target multiples. The 12-month case to $55 only requires the noise to quiet down.

Recap: I have a $76 target (in 18-24 months) and $55 near-term target (12 months).

data.title: Voya Financial: Right On Fundamentals, But The Re-Rating Is Delayed (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Elevator Thesis

Voya Financial (VOYA) is just not a simple re-rating story anymore.

The investment case is no longer the same since my last coverage on November 12, 2025. Voya's fundamentals continue to improve. The market, however, is not yet ready to give a higher multiple to it. This is mainly due to quarterly volatility, especially in the Employee Benefits segment.

Interestingly, Voya is a staggering $1.1 trillion health and wealth platform today, with its Retirement and Investment management businesses. This has led to a more fee-based earnings profile, which has been underpinned by highly defined contribution inflows and accelerating retirement assets.

The FY 25 results somewhat showcase this strength. The adjusted EPS bumped 22% annually to $8.85, driven by robust retirement growth and capital generation.

Let's zoom in on Q4 and assess where the problem is. Adjusted EPS for Q4 clocked in at $1.94, which was below the estimated $2.09. The difference was caused by the increased compensation costs and volatility in medical stop-loss.

Well, these are not structural issues, but they add enough uncertainty to hold back a clean re-rating.

Furthermore, Voya is currently trading at around $72-$73 with 7.8x forward earnings. Such valuation is normally assigned to weaker insurers. It is not for a business that can generate an 18-19% return on equity, with robust cash flow and capital returns.

The gap is an outcome of the inconsistency of overall earnings, and that's what is keeping the valuation in check.

Therefore, I assign VOYA a 'Hold' rating right now.

The long-term story is still intact. A re-rating will require a couple of clean quarters and not just steady growth.

Strong Retirement Engine Still Underappreciated

VOYA's Q4 25 reinforced that the Retirement business is not a weakness.

The quarter's EPS miss was driven by elevated compensation costs and Employee Benefits volatility. Retirement itself is pretty stable and is surely not a contributing factor in the downside.

More to the point, FY 25 showed steady compounding as organic Defined Contribution net inflows were at $28.2 billion. These were backed by continued employer participation and consistent retirement contribution behavior.

These are not like traditional asset management flows since they are built into payroll systems. That causes them to be cyclic and less sensitive to market cycles.

Moreover, stability is more meaningful when scale is kept in consideration. The OneAmerica acquisition contributed about $60 billion of retirement assets during the year. This pushed Voya's total retirement and investment platform to over $1.1 trillion.

At this level, the business is already operating at scale, where efficiency and stability are more important than sheer growth.

Notably, this growth has not been attained at the cost of profitability.

The retirement margins have remained at the high 30%-40% range, which is a positive sign that scale is under control.

Essentially, Retirement is doing what a good-quality pension and retirement platform is supposed to do. The problem with this is that this strength still exists within total earnings, which the market does not fully trust yet.

Hence, Retirement is not getting a higher multiple in the market.

Employee Benefits is the Missing Piece in Consistency

Employee Benefits is where the earnings consistency starts to lose smoothness.

Again, that's visible in Q4. The segment had an operating loss of approximately $10 million, although full-year results were still positive at $152 million.

That contrast is important as it shows that the underlying business is not worsening, but the quarterly path is still bumpy enough to upset consolidated outcomes.

The primary cause of this mismatch comes from medical stop-loss claims. They are simply unpredictable.

In Q4, reserve strengthening added about $37 million. Interestingly, this pressure was enough to weigh on the segment efficiency and the overall earnings sentiment.

In the meantime, management is working. The 24% increment of the January 2026 cohort is a marker of continued pricing discipline.

The flow of pricing changes is slow, and actions and results are still not linked. The market will eventually react when higher rates are maintained without the pressure of retention or adverse selection. Until then, doubt will remain imprinted in the expectations.

Yes, Retirement in itself is powerful enough to sustain an increased multiple. That said, Employee Benefits is too volatile to view that strength in any clear way. This forces the market to consider the inconsistent consolidated earnings.

This near-term dynamic remains unchanged even with the support programs such as the latest TELUS Health partnership. It is a good long-term move that can enhance health outcomes of employees and reduce claims in the long run. However, it won't stabilize the earnings in the coming few quarters.

Capital Return: Strong Base, Limited Upside Driver

Voya continued to generate solid excess capital in FY 25, coming in around $775 million. That amount of cash production shows a high-quality earnings base, which is backed by returns on equity in the high teens.

Notably, this is not stressed capital creation. It is predictable even during a quarter such as Q4, where the EPS is lower than anticipated.

This robustness is flowing into shareholder returns. In early 2026, Voya completed $150 million in buybacks and has committed another $150 million for Q2. These buybacks are highly efficient at around 7.8x forward earnings. Every dollar invested is returned with a double-digit profit through the low valuation.

This forms a decent base. Shareholder returns are steady even in a tight market, along with a 2.5% dividend yield. That is why the stock fails to break materially lower in the face of earnings volatility.

Despite the announced buybacks of $300 million in Q1 and Q2, the stock has not gotten out of its range of $69 to $74. The capital return is still not strong enough to offset the earnings unevenness.

The balance sheet also has a structural signal. Voya has excess capital with RBC ratios at about 413% as opposed to an estimated 375%.

It is a strength, but it also means that there are few avenues of reinvestment in the short term. Consequently, the capital is being returned naturally as opposed to being aggressively utilized in growth programs.

Capital returns are supporting the stock; however, they do not address the clarity of earnings. It is also more of a stabilizer than a re-rating catalyst.

Valuation Discussion

VOYA continues to trade sideways, and that is the clearest signal at the moment.

VOYA has remained mostly in the range of $69-$74. It has tried both ends of that range, yet it has been unable to maintain a breakout. The market is yet to determine a higher multiple, although the earnings are on the rise.

The stock is in the middle of the range of about $72-$73 (as of writing). It is not distressed or in re-rating. A crucial question is whether Voya will be able to provide stability in the quarterly earnings.

This hesitation is supported by an FY26 consensus EPS of $9.57. The growth is consistent but not high enough to change the expectations significantly. The market is not anticipating a downturn but does desire a clear earnings trajectory.

The stock appears cheap at an estimated 7.8x forward earnings. This is particularly true due to a high capital generation and 18-19% ROE. ROE is not, however, being priced in isolation by the market. It is discounting earnings volatility, mainly driven by Employee Benefits. This is why the multiple stays are capped.

Let's assess the scenario framework.

First is the bear case scenario ($60-$65). Employee Benefits remains volatile and pricing measures partially counter the claims pressure; then the earnings will continue to be uneven.

Meanwhile, in the base case of the stock at $72-$75, Retirement pays off and capital returns remain constant. Employee Benefits only enhance gradually, yet they introduce a bit of lumpiness.

Under the bull case ($88-$95), assuming that Employee Benefits stabilize and Retirement compounds, then earnings are predictable once again. At that point, the market ceases to be volatility-conscious and begins to pay a premium on quality.

The consistency of the path is more important than the direction of earnings. The market is ready to embrace growth, but it has yet to come to terms with the imbalanced execution.

Until then, the stock will be range-bound, with a bias to the base case.

Risks to the Thesis and Takeaway

There are a few things that can delay the story from playing out.

First is the timing of Employee Benefits. When the effects of the 24% pricing actions take more time to become visible, then the earnings may remain disproportionate longer than anticipated. It's also going to slow down the improvement due to retention pressure or adverse selection. That would simply keep the stock stuck in its current range for longer.

Then there's market exposure. Retirement and Investment Management are fairly stable, though the fee income remains dependent on equity markets and bond markets. A sudden change in markets may temporarily decelerate the earnings momentum, despite underlying flows remaining constant.

The third one is capital deployment. Voya is generating strong excess capital, but most of it is still going back to shareholders through buybacks. With limited reinvestment opportunities remaining, growth will have to rely more on financial engineering than on actual operational expansion. That basically restricts the extent to which the multiple can move up.

Additionally, the stock may remain range-bound as long as investors continue to concentrate on previous quarterly volatility. Valuation recovery in such a case might lag behind the business.

In general, these risks do not alter the course of the business. They mainly affect the speed of re-rating. The retirement engine is solid, the generation of capital is sound, and long-term structure is maintained.

In the case of current holders, it is not an urgent reason to exit. For new investors, it is prudent to wait till the cleaner quarters, where the earnings will better represent the core business.

data.title: Applied Materials: A Picks And Shovels Play On The AI Chip Revolution
data.description:
Thesis

Applied Materials, Inc. (AMAT) is, in my view, one of the best ways to get exposure to the AI infrastructure buildout without betting on a single horse. The company is situated in the center of the circuit fabrication universe, selling the tools each and every modern fab relies on. My perspective is supported by three arguments: the company's dominant position as materials engineering leader for angstrom-era chips, a financial position that offers both strong margins and ample capital returns to shareholders, and a multiyear tailwind from boosting DRAM and advanced packaging demand.

However, risk is high with this stock, and valuation is a true debate at current levels. Applied Materials has significant upsides only if current demand for AI driven equipment is sustained; if the cycle drags or if policy headwinds intensify, excess margin for error will remain absent. For buy-and-hold investors willing to tolerate near-term policy knives and the cyclical semiconductor industry, I give Applied Materials a cautious buy score.

Company Overview

Applied Materials was formed in 1967 as one of the first companies built specifically to design and market manufacturing equipment to chipmakers. Today it is the world’s largest supplier of equipment to manufacture semiconductor wafers. Headquartered in Santa Clara, California, its product range includes systems for deposition, etch, inspection, and process control, alongside services and software that enable chip and advanced display production.

In the real world, if a foundry or memory maker is looking to scale down transistors, stack memory layers, or wrap nanosheets in exotic metal gates, Applied Materials is probably the company providing a key piece of that puzzle. It has three reporting segments (Semiconductor Systems, Applied Global Services, and Display), but the Semiconductor Division is clearly the growth driver in the modern era, particularly as AI and GPU computing equipment require ever more complex chip designs.

Qualitative and Quantitative Analysis

What surprises me most about Applied is the quality of its business model. Over the last two years, Applied has averaged an operating margin of about 29%, making it one of the more efficient companies in the semiconductor equipment space. This is not accidental; it speaks to the years of process know-how and strong customer relationships that produce significant switching costs. Once a fab concludes a deposition or etch tool into a high-volume manufacturing line, the removal of the system is prohibitively costly and dangerous.

Free cash flow is telling a similar story. The trailing two-year average free cash flow margin is around 22%, providing much-needed flexibility. It allows management to spend heavily on R&D, a necessity in a business where the physics changes with each new node, but also to reward shareholders. Over the last ten years, Applied Materials has returned almost 90% of its cumulative free cash flow via buybacks and dividends.

Dividend per share has compounded at around 18% a year during this time, and the last 15% increase, to $0.53 a quarter, extends an unbroken nine-year run of increases. I take that as a sign of genuine belief in the sustainability of the cash generation engine.

Finally, the ROIC (return on invested capital) figure is clearly the most illuminating. Applied Materials' five-year average ROIC is approximately 47%, an incredible rate suggesting that the management team always has better than average projects to invest in. Considering the industry is so cyclical, maintaining that type of capital efficiency from one cycle to the next is truly remarkable.

The near‑term revenue picture is dominated by AI‑driven memory capex. Fiscal Q1 2026 was the first quarter to deliver a new record for DRAM equipment—$1.75 billion of revenue—and the company has stated publicly that it expects DRAM to be its fastest-growing segment for calendar 2026. Wolfe Research sees Applied Materials’ DRAM revenue rising around 34% in calendar year 2027—driven by the company’s solid deposition and conductor etch positioning. NAND recovery, though, is less than half those levels - roughly 10% for the same year - pointing towards a more constrained competitive standing at this end of the industry.

Consensus analyst estimates show revenue of roughly $32.5 billion and earnings of roughly $9.2 billion by FY28, with a few more bullish analysts forecasting as much as $35 billion in revenue and $9.8 billion in earnings over the same time horizon. These estimates are aggressive, but not completely inconsistent with the ongoing AI-driven equipment upcycle I observe. I anchor my own valuation to the low end of the consensus EPS band to account for the cyclical nature of the business and inherent capex-reduction risk.

Key Competitors

The three competitors I pay the closest attention to are Lam Research, ASML, and Tokyo Electron.

In terms of deposition and etch, Lam Research (LRCX) is the main competitor, especially in NAND, where it holds a larger share. In terms of EUV lithography, ASML's (ASML) monopoly position is so unique that it is not a straight head-to-head fight, more of a natural complement, but ASML's share of every advanced fab dollar is a restraint on what Applied Materials can capture. Tokyo Electron (TOELY) is the third member of this competitive triumvirate with broad-line equipment offerings, including some particular wins with coater developer tools and some etch apps.

Applied Materials differential advantage over all three is the size of its portfolio and the depth of its materials engineering expertise, which will be more and more critical as the transistor architecture becomes more three-dimensional and multi-material at the angstrom scale. While Lam and Tokyo Electron dominate some of the modules, and ASML is a fixture at the lithography layer, Applied Materials is increasingly at the focal point of the fab’s “materials stack”.

Catalysts and Recent News

The biggest recent catalyst came on April 8 when Applied Materials announced two new fabrication systems built specifically for 2-nanometer and sub-2-nm gate all-around (GAA) transistors. The newly introduced Precision Selective Nitride PECVD platform deposits a protective silicon nitride layer that maintains shallow trench isolation integrity and improves performance per watt through its reduction of parasitic capacitance. Likewise, the Trillium ALD system encloses silicon nanosheets with complex metal gate stacks, allowing chipmakers to tailor transistor performance for various AI workloads.

These already manage the process of material deposition down to individual atom-scale levels, exactly at the point the angstrom era demands. The stock jumped about 9% on the announcement day, and the 30-day return before mid-April was over 18%, indicating the market had expected good news.

On the analyst side, the picture is mixed. Wolfe Research, for example, has raised its price target on the company to $500 from $450 based on the DRAM opportunity and has rated the stock an Outperform. MoffettNathanson , on the other hand, lowered its target on the company to $214 from $231, mainly due to compressed valuation multiples, but still maintains a Buy rating. This $214.00 to $500.00 spread really shows how divided the street is on this name. Wolf’s target is high up in the bullish section of the camp, and MoffettNathanson is more conservative on the company.

Other positives: a new third-party logistics alliance using UPS’s Taiwan hub to distribute in Asia, new U.S. government support for U.S. supply chains with the CHIPS Act, and the aforementioned dividend hike. Negatives: Ongoing Chinese export-control uncertainty is the main overhang. Unspecific management guidance stated that a large % of g/u orders & revenue come from the China exposed fabs. Further tightening of US export controls or China's retaliatory actions could substantially impact the business and margins.

Valuation

That's where the disagreement is between the bulls and bears. On the one hand, Applied trades at about 33 times forward earnings, with the stock recently trading around $400. The consensus average analyst price target is about $435, suggesting only a low to mid single-digit percentage upside - implied certainly not what one would call mind-boggling for a cyclical growth stock.

One of the more popular discounted cash flow models, which assumes the long-term growth and margin component not to be particularly aggressive, suggests a fair value of about $190, implying a significant overvaluation of the stock. I think this model uses far too conservative assumptions regarding long-term trends and undervalues the structural secular trend toward higher IT CapEx and the AI-driven data center infrastructure buildout, as well as the robustness of Applied Materials’ competitive moat. I think the combination of sustained AI-CapEx-driven strength and the company's deep-quality earnings profile supports a premium to the more conservative DCF estimates even if the stock isn't cheap today.

My framework takes the consensus 2028 earnings estimate, which is $9.2 billion (adjusted EPS), and applies a 25-28 times earnings multiple, which seems reasonable given the high ROIC capital goods compounder in a structurally growing semiconductor ecosystem, and discounts back at 10%, representing the current elevated cost of capital and the cyclical risk of the business. That math yields a fair value range of approximately $375-$420 per share, implying that the stock is about fairly valued today and not cheap or expensive. Certainly, a bull case above $450 requires either more than expected DRAM and advanced packaging growth, or some degree of multiple expansion, both of which seem likely but are by no means certain

Risks

Three risks could alter my analysis. One would be a significant tightening of China export restrictions, or a retaliatory move that would significantly erode revenue and reduce margins. A second issue would be a slowing of overall semiconductor capex, a risk that is intrinsic in a cyclical industry that would disproportionately impact equipment vendors and reduce multiples. A third risk is customer concentration: large foundry and memory customers’ purchasing behavior creates lumpiness in orders and additional earnings volatility. A significant increase in any of these three factors would cause me to reassess the thesis.

Conclusion

Applied Materials is a best-in-class business riding a major secular tailwind. Its margins, cash flow conversion, and ROIC are all best in class, and the latest angstrom era tool launches cement its technological leadership at exactly the time when chipmakers need that expertise the most. The valuation is not cheap on most traditional measures, but I think the quality of the franchise and the multi-year AI-driven equipment upcycle mean the premium is warranted. However, the valuation already assumes a good deal of optimism; there is little margin for error should the AI capex cycle temper or policy catalysts turn sour.

For multi-year-horizon investors comfortable with semiconductor cyclicality and who have the stomach for policy noise about China, Applied Materials stays a core holding.
