data.title: ATI Inc.: Why I Remain Bullish On Aerospace And Defense Growth
data.description:
In January, I marked ATI Inc. (ATI) a strong buy with a $163.13 bullish price target. Since then, the stock has reached my price target. That may be somewhat remarkable given the market turmoil over the past weeks and aerospace and defense names definitely not being favored by the market. With the price targets reached and earnings coming up later this month, it is a good moment to revisit ATI Inc.

Potential Risks For ATI With Offsets

The increase in oil prices is currently driving up energy prices and inflation. I believe that as a critical provider of material alloys such as titanium alloys, nickel-based alloys, superalloys, and forged components, ATI has a strong position to pass through costs using raw material surcharges and escalation clauses. The business is highly energy intensive, but those can also be passed through price resets and broader escalation clauses. So, the impact of higher raw material costs, energy costs, and inflation can generally be passed through with some lag, causing a temporary margin contraction. However, the cost side of the business should be shielded extremely well also because businesses like ATI operate under long-term agreements with their suppliers.

On the demand side, there are some risks. ATI generates 70% of its sales from aerospace and defense and 30% from other industries. Those industries already had quite a difficult time, and with economic growth under pressure, the revenues could be under pressure. The stock market has reached new highs, but it seems to completely ignore that GDP growth outlooks have weakened. That is an obvious risk, because while the stock market ignores it, a reduction in the growth outlook may result in lower growth in ATI’s end markets. In aerospace and defense, I do believe that defense demand will remain stable. In Aerospace, I do not expect sales to original equipment manufacturers to be affected for the simple reason that we have not seen any tweaks to airplane production rates. There is, however, a risk that we should keep in mind, and that is that airlines have started flying less, and that also may result in lower demand for commercial MRO. As utilization decreases or active fleets shrink, demand for parts and services also declines unless airlines opportunistically pull forward maintenance events.

Overall, I do believe that while there may be some soft spots even in aerospace and defense, there should be substantial growth ahead.

Analysts Expect Modest Revenue Growth With Strong EPS Growth

For the first quarter, analysts are expecting ATI to report $1.19 billion in revenues, marking 3.8% sales growth. We note that over the past six months, the estimates have come down by 2.5%, and by 1.8% over the past three months, with a 0.4% increase over the past month. So, there is no expected major impact due to the war, and that shows the resilience of ATI. A growth of 3.8% does not sound impressive, but we note that aerospace and defense companies tend to realize their growth from a lower base driven by end-of-year increases in deliveries of products, parts, and materials, leaving the subsequent quarter lower on deliverable inventories.

Earnings per share are estimated to be $0.88, marking 22.3% growth, with estimates having increased 2.3% over the past six months, 3.2% over the past three months, and 0.9% over the past month. So, we are also not seeing any impact on earnings per share from potential higher costs. The company will report Q1 2026 earnings on the 30th of April, and it will be interesting to see whether they have any comments on demand and the cost side of the business, but it is looking a lot like ATI is well-positioned to absorb any costs, and demand remains robust.

When it comes to beating analyst estimates, we note that the company’s track record on revenues is not the best, with six misses over the past 12 quarters, including a miss in the prior quarter. On earnings per share, the track record is more robust with one miss over the past twelve quarters, and the last reading that missed estimates was in Q3 2024.

ATI Margins And Free Cash Flow Drive Upside

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

We note that ATI is not cheap at current prices since it trades at 28x EV/EBITDA, and the company has been trading at twice its median trading multiple. However, it seems that ATI has made a step change from trading like a material cyclical name to a cyclical aerospace and defense name. It is still cyclical, but it is substantially better to be valued as an aerospace and defense company. Given the high exposure to aerospace and defense, I do believe it makes sense to be valued in line with the aerospace peers, and that would indicate a $200.83 price target with 22% upside, which is enough to maintain my strong buy rating. We also note that the company’s price level suggests that it now has a tendency to trade in line with peers.

For ATI, we see that the operating leverage has been kicking in, and as a result, cash flows are sharply improving. This looks like one of the best operating leverage plays I have come across in the industry. Capital intensity is set to moderate in the years ahead and fall to 4% of revenues by 2028. I do expect the company to maintain a sizable share repurchase program. In terms of net debt leverage, we note that the company is in an extremely strong position, and that may also mean that it will refinance some of its net debt maturities since it does not really need to bring the leverage down. I have modeled that they will indeed repay the maturities in 2027, which are the biggest block of debt obligations in the coming years, and it shows that even then the company should be able to maintain a cash position from which it will rebound to around $350 million in the year after.

Conclusion: ATI Operating Leverage Strength Shines

I believe that ATI is well positioned to offset any increase in cost it may experience due to cost inflation and higher raw material costs, which in turn may be padded through long-term supply agreements. In terms of demand, I also do not foresee a major demand shake-up, and in defense, we may even see some incremental upside as utilization of military aircraft has increased. As a result, I am maintaining my strong buy rating.

data.title: Ardagh Metal Packaging: Execution Holds, Tailwinds Build, Valuation Undemanding
data.description:
After three years without a rating, we are back to comment on and analyze Ardagh Metal Packaging (AMBP). The company is a global leader in beverage cans and a best-in-class specialty mix. Its mother company, Ardagh Group, performed a recapitalization on 12 November 2025, which included the mandatory exchange of all outstanding senior unsecured bonds for partial equity in a newly restructured entity. This will likely prevent any further negative press for Ardagh Metal Packaging, which is a separate, ring-fenced entity.

Why are we positive?

Our decision to take a follow-up note was prompted by discussions at the Euronext STAR Conference, where Zignago Vetro’s IR highlighted persistently elevated energy costs across the sector. This was due to the escalation in the Middle East. While the bear case may frame this as another transition year, we believe the company continues to execute effectively, as evidenced by a further EBITDA beat in Q4 and initial Q1 guidance slightly ahead of Wall Street consensus. To add numbers, trends were positive in 4Q, but the company reported solid adjusted EBITDA of $166 million, above the Street average of $157 million, and adjusted EPS of $0.03, above the Street average of $0.02.

Fig. 1

Ardagh Metal Packaging is exposed to changes in energy prices. However, we report that the company's annual report states that it has "an active hedging strategy to fix a significant proportion of our energy costs through contractual arrangements directly with our suppliers. Our policy is to purchase natural gas and electricity by entering into forward price-fixing arrangements with suppliers for the majority of our anticipated requirements for the year ahead and for further diminishing portions of our anticipated requirements for subsequent years." The company was heavily impacted by the supply shock caused by the Russia-Ukraine war. While there is no disclosure on energy hedge in Ardagh Metal Packaging's slide, particularly compared with the transparency typically seen in the aviation sector (International Consolidated Airlines Group at 62% and Deutsche Lufthansa AG at 77%), here at the Lab, we believe the company has experienced past volatility and maintains an active and significant protection level.

The company expects volume to grow in LatAm and the EU; however, it reports softer volume contracts in NA. Last year, the company still outperformed the industry. However, Ardagh's story could become more interesting as we head into 2027, with new contract wins. We believe this sub-segment will grow faster than population growth. This is because: 1) the use of metal, which is fully recyclable, reduces the need for virgin raw materials, and 2) the use of metal enables energy savings based on limited raw material extraction and lower melting furnaces' energy consumption. Here at the Lab, we believe this is not well displayed in the company's communication. Megatrends and ESG support Ardagh Metal Packaging's demand. This is also combined with earnings stability, given a pass-through provision on input costs. We well understood that in the Zignago Vetro meeting.

Looking at Q4 FCF, the company delivered $172 million, compared to a Wall Street consensus expectation of $254 million. Net debt/LTM EBITDA is now at ~5.3x. This is a high number. However, the company remains with a green bond in 2025. This included the redemption of the preferred shares, so there will be a benefit from paying a lower dividend in 2026, which a higher cash interest payment will slightly offset. The net benefit is $10 million annually. The company maintains a yearly dividend of $0.4/share (paid quarterly). In 2025, Ardagh's dividend payment was $262 million and was not matched by its FCF generation, which reached €232 million, as shown in Fig. . 2.

Bears might raise questions about Ardagh Metal Packaging's dividend sustainability. However, the company has reiterated its commitment to its dividend policy. At the Lab, we believe the company may reduce its payout (given a dividend yield of almost 10%), but a reiteration of the payment suggests they can sustain this commitment. A reduction might also support a deleveraging process (given a net debt-to-EBITDA ratio of 5.3x). Having said that, we can value the company using either a dividend growth model or an FCF yield; in either case, the result is similar to 10%. This means a buy rating.

Fig. 2

Lastly, on a positive note, the company was subject to heavy short interest in the past. In late 2025, a few well-known hedge fund managers entered Ardagh. In particular, Arini Capital Management initiated a new position of 11.25 million shares. This was followed by Monarch Alternative Capital, which also added 5.35 million shares.

Valuation

Looking ahead, the company guided 2026 adjusted EBITDA to $750–$775 million, with Q1 adjusted EBITDA of $160–$170 million (Fig. . 2). The company reported solid results from North America in the quarter. In 2026, we expect volume/mix in Brazil to be up low-to-mid-single digits, with North America declining (despite positive Q1 evolution) due to contract resets and footprint changes. Europe's growth will likely grow at 3%. Here at the Lab, we are also positive this year, given the football World Cup, which might provide a positive tailwind. Even applying the low end of the company's EBITDA guidance of $750 million, with a 5-year historical EV/EBITDA multiple of 9.5x, this would result in an enterprise value of $7.12 billion. Considering the debt ($3.9 billion), there is a 32% upside to the current estimates. This is also combined with a 10% dividend or a deleverage path of the same amount. Therefore, we reinstated Ardagh Metal Packaging's rating to buy and set a target price of $5.6 per share.

Risks

The company's key risks include supply/demand imbalance and higher energy costs. Additionally, Ardagh Metal Packaging might be impacted by higher maintenance CAPEX. The company carries a net leverage of 5.3x. Equity is highly sensitive to small changes in enterprise value and the interest-rate environment. Dividend sustainability is another risk because it was not supported by FCF generation. Any operational weakness could force a cut, which would likely cause a sharp sell-off in the stock and cause a multiple de-rating. As a controlled company (76% owned by Ardagh Group), the company has limited rights and recourse for minority shareholders. While secular trends are favorable, a recession could impact volumes.

Conclusion

Overall, this is a well-run company with supportive trends ahead, solid shareholder remuneration, and a not-pricey valuation. Hedge fund sentiment seems to revert, and this is usually the first factor to swing sell-side optimism in Wall Street equity research in the medium term. We reinitiated the company with a buy rating.

data.title: BDCs That Put Their Money Where Their Mouth Is
data.description:
The BDC sector (BIZD) has landed on the chopping block of many bears.

Given the intensity at which bearish headlines get pumped across the financial media channels, we are most likely well aware of the "so-called" issues:

  • Many hidden cockroaches such as Tricolor and First Brands.
  • Huge exposure to SaaS, which is likely to struggle due to the advancing AI.
  • Exodus of capital from private BDCs and the corresponding liquidity caps.
  • Opaque valuation methods.

There are many more, but these are probably the main ones.

Most of these risks started to emerge from mid-January this year:

On a YTD basis, the index is down by ~7%, which, considering the pre-existing discounts, has contributed to a sector median discount of 27%. The market-cap weighted index (RJ BDC Index) is down ~11%.

In the meantime, almost all BDC managers have characterized this dynamic as a significant disconnect from the underlying reality or the underlying financial situation. This is at least how I interpret their comments in Q4 2025 earnings calls and in some select conferences.

Personally, I also see the situation in a similar way - i.e., a huge buying opportunity. You can find more details in my recent piece here: BDCs: The Time To Buy This 14% Yielding Sector Is Now

Now, given the combination of a) deep-value level discounts and b) strong conviction about the disconnect between value and share price, it would be only logical if we saw heavy insider buying activity.

Notable insider buying activity would, in my view, put a cherry on top of a specific (discounted) BDC's investment case.

Conversely, if there were no insider buying involved, especially in the case of significantly lower share price and deep discount, I would probably question the validity of BDCs' investment cases. I'm not saying that I would sell, but at least I would reexamine the position and perhaps trim the exposure a bit.

Without further ado, let's take a look at 20 BDCs and their insider activity.

The analysis

There are a couple of aspects I would like to underscore before you see the table that I have developed.

Ticker: 20 BDCs that, in my view, are well-known.

Acquisitions: Net buying activity of the selected BDCs over the past 3-month period (Jan 15 - April 15). Note, from all 20 BDCs, there was only 1 case in which some selling was done: BlackRock TCP Capital Corp (TCPC).

Insiders: The number of unique insiders (persons) who have made the purchases.

Average: Acquisition amount divided by number of insiders.

3M price: BDC's share price movement in the past 3-month period.

Ticker Acquisitions, $ Insiders, # Average, $ 3M price, %
TSLX 10.8M 6 1.8M -15%
HTGC 2.8M 11 255K -17.3%
ARCC 943K 4 236K -9.6%
TRIN 554K 3 185K -3.6%
MSDL 340K 4 85K -9.1%
TCPC 237K 4 59K -25.6%
PFLT 172K 2 86K -10.3%
KBDC 138K 1 138K -3.8%
OBDC 113K 1 113K -8.6%
FSK 75K 2 37K -24.1%
CSWC 73K 2 36K 1.1%
BXSL 67K 3 22K -6.1%
MAIN - - - -9.9%
FDUS - - - -7.3%
CION - - - -21.2%
CCAP - - - -9.7%
GAIN - - - -10.0%
GLAD - - - 8.3%
TPVG - - - -20.3%
HRZN - - - -29.6%

P.S. Insider trading data is gathered from SecForm4.

Key takeaways

From the analysis or data points above, I think there are two key takeaways - other than what is very obvious from the table, like the fact that the situation for Sixth Street Specialty (TSLX) is extremely bullish - which we can extract and factor into our investment decision making.

1. The purchases for really heavily discounted BDCs have been rare.

To be honest, what has surprised me is the fact that many of the most discounted vehicles have not been subject to at least above average insider buying. In fact, most of them have not seen any insider buying at all.

For example, Horizon Technology Finance (HRZN), TriplePoint Venture Growth (TPVG) and CION Investment Corp (CION) trade at P/NAV of 0.65x, 0.59x and 0.52x, respectively, all well below the sector median of 0.73x. These BDCs are also among the ones that have experienced the biggest share price drops in the past 3 months. For FSK KKR Capital Corp. (FSK), the story is quite similar, although there has been a tiny bit of purchasing made.

The way we can read this is that the insiders seeing the opportunity to buy their businesses almost at 50 cents on the dollar have still decided, for some reason, to sit on the sidelines. It is a bit speculative, but one potential explanation could be related to the expectation of a further sell-off, which could potentially be followed right after the publication of Q1 2026 reports.

2. The purchases have been notable for discounted internal BDCs.

Most of my closest followers who are interested in BDCs are probably already aware of my bullish stance on internally managed BDCs.

In the table I have included all of them: Trinity Capital (TRIN), Capital Southwest (CSWC), Main Street Capital (MAIN) and Hercules Capital (HTGC).

We can see that neither MAIN nor CSWC have made significant purchases (MAIN's insiders have not bought at all). I think that we should not take this as a big negative. Two reasons:

  1. MAIN and CSWC are both the most expensive BDCs in the sector with P/NAV of 1.60x and 1.35x, respectively.
  2. Their share prices have not experienced a violent correction.

What this, however, implies is that the upside potential from the price gains perspective is likely limited here (due to no deep discounts), which is completely fine with me. The main criteria for me are a) durable income and b) resilient NAV, and then only comes the "c"), which is decent price performance.

For TRIN and HTGC the insider buying activity has been significant.

Let me quickly unpack this.

TRIN's case is a bit tricky.

On the one hand, the share price has not moved that much in the past 3-month period. Plus, the current P/NAV is 1.12x, which is materially above the sector median.

On the other hand, we have to appreciate the fact that TRIN is heavily discounted on a relative basis. Namely, internally traded BDCs MAIN, CSWC and HTGC trade at much higher multiples (see above for MAIN and CSWC; HTGC trades at P/NAV of 1.22x).

At the same time TRIN has below-average SaaS exposure, below-average debt levels, below-average PIK and non-accruals, and, importantly, growing NII per share Q4 statistics, which was not the case for other peers (see more details on TRIN in my article here). So, it is only logical that the BDC insiders welcomed the slightly decreased share price and reacted accordingly.

HTGC is a more straightforward case.

The most likely reason why the insiders have really loaded up on HTGC shares is related to the ~17% sell-off, which has been comparable to that of TPVG, HRZN and FSK - all BDCs with surging non-accruals, dividend cuts, etc.

The only rational explanation for this is HTGC's massive exposure to SaaS names. Apart from this, the key fundamentals remain in rock-solid shape (similar to TRIN) with, for example, non-accruals at 0.2% of portfolio cost, debt-to-equity of 1.03x, portfolio company interest coverage of 4.9x and base dividend coverage level of 120%. All these metrics are significantly better than for the sector average player (and, more importantly, very robust on an absolute level). See more details on HTGC's case in my ticker-specific article here.

So it is only logical that if the management thinks that the SaaS-driven selloff is detached from reality, then they would be heavy buyers of the ~17% dip opportunity.

Final remarks

The overall BDC insider transaction activity sends very bullish signals. The fact that none of my selected 20 BDCs have been subject to net selling activity speaks volumes.

However, I would be careful about BDCs that trade at steep discounts (close to 40% to 50% below NAV) but have not been scooped up by their insiders. FSK, HRZN, TPVG and CION are the main ones.

Finally, it is encouraging to see bullish confirmation signs on TSLX, TRIN, HTGC, Ares Capital (ARCC) and even Morgan Stanley Direct Lending (MSDL), on which I have active buy ratings.

In a nutshell, this is another structural data point that argues in favor of BDCs and supports my strategic recommendation to go big on BDCs.
